title: The High Price You Pay to Get on Board Hot Companies Before They Go Public
title_cn: 在热门公司上市前入股所需付出的高昂代价

paragraphs.cn: 2026年1月30日上午9:00（美国东部时间）

paragraphs.en: “I keep hearing about ‘democratizing’ access to private assets,” says John DiCarlo, a Wall Street Journal reader. “But nearly all of it seems to be on the fee sharks’ terms.”
paragraphs.cn: 《华尔街日报》读者约翰·迪卡洛说：“我一直听说‘民主化’私人资产的获取途径，但几乎所有这些似乎都受制于那些收费高昂的‘鲨鱼’。”

paragraphs.en: DiCarlo, an engineer in the Washington, D.C., area, recently reached out to me because he has been trying—with no luck—for at least two years to buy a stake in private companies before they launch an initial public offering of their shares.
paragraphs.cn: 迪卡洛是华盛顿特区的一名工程师，他最近联系了我，因为他至少两年来一直试图在私营公司首次公开募股（IPO）前购买其股份，但都未能成功。

paragraphs.en: For me, his dilemma epitomizes both the potential and pitfalls of investing in private assets. Who wouldn’t want to get an early stake in the companies creating the future? But the democratization of private markets is about as problematic as the democratization of former dictatorships. We have a long way to go before individual investors can participate with ease and at a competitive price.
paragraphs.cn: 对我而言，他的困境集中体现了投资私人资产的潜力和陷阱。谁不想在创造未来的公司中获得早期股份呢？但私人市场的民主化与前独裁政权的民主化一样充满问题。在个人投资者能够轻松以有竞争力的价格参与之前，我们还有很长的路要走。

paragraphs.en: Firms such as EquityZen, Forge Global, Hiive and Nasdaq Private Market have made considerable progress in making information available on their websites and apps. For hot, “pre-IPO” companies expected to go public soon, such as SpaceX, OpenAI or Anthropic, you can track share prices over time, see what’s available, and seek to transact in shares or in other investment vehicles that track their performance.
paragraphs.cn: EquityZen、Forge Global、Hiive和纳斯达克私人市场等公司在通过其网站和应用程序提供信息方面取得了显著进展。对于像SpaceX、OpenAI或Anthropic这样预计很快上市的热门“IPO前”公司，你可以追踪其股价随时间的变化，查看可用的股份，并寻求交易股份或跟踪其表现的其他投资工具。

paragraphs.en: But you’re more likely to be struck by lightning than you are to buy shares directly in most pre-IPO companies.
paragraphs.cn: 但你直接购买大多数IPO前公司股份的可能性，比被闪电击中的可能性还要小。

paragraphs.en: Regulations generally discourage private companies from taking on more than 2,000 shareholders of record. And you have to be a so-called accredited investor with at least $1 million in net assets, excluding your primary residence, or at least $200,000 in individual or $300,000 in joint income.
paragraphs.cn: 监管规定通常不鼓励私营公司拥有超过2000名登记股东。而且，你必须是一名所谓的“合格投资者”，拥有至少100万美元的净资产（不包括主要住所），或者个人年收入至少20万美元，或夫妻共同年收入至少30万美元。

paragraphs.en: The most common form of access is often called a special-purpose vehicle. Some managers of SPVs, knowing they’re among the few ways to buy into coveted pre-IPO companies, charge fees that would make a bandit blush.
paragraphs.cn: 最常见的获取方式通常被称为特殊目的载体（SPV）。一些SPV的管理者深知他们是少数能够投资备受追捧的IPO前公司的方式之一，因此收取的费用高得连强盗都会脸红。

paragraphs.en: SPVs can bear upfront fees that could hit 5% to 12% or more, say industry executives. Some also charge performance fees that take 10% to 30% of any gains. The highest costs are hard to overcome, says Tom Callahan, chief executive of Nasdaq Private Market. “You better hope that company 10X’es or your return will be eroded away in fees,” he says.
paragraphs.cn: 行业高管表示，SPV可能收取5%到12%甚至更高的前期费用。有些还会收取业绩费，占任何收益的10%到30%。纳斯达克私人市场首席执行官汤姆·卡拉汉表示，最高的成本很难克服。“你最好希望那家公司能增长10倍，否则你的回报就会被费用侵蚀殆尽，”他说。

paragraphs.en: Less-expensive alternatives are becoming available, but they still aren’t cheap compared with the costs of investing in public markets.
paragraphs.cn: 成本较低的替代方案正在出现，但与投资公开市场的成本相比，它们仍然不便宜。

paragraphs.en: Hiive, for instance, offers single-stock SPVs that hold shares in such private companies as Databricks or xAI, with no management or performance fees. Purchase fees run from 1.85% (on amounts above $1 million) to 5%. If you sell, you’ll pay a commission of up to 4.9%.
paragraphs.cn: 例如，Hiive提供单一股票SPV，持有Databricks或xAI等私营公司的股份，不收取管理费或业绩费。购买费用从1.85%（针对100万美元以上的金额）到5%不等。如果你出售，将支付高达4.9%的佣金。

paragraphs.en: The Intelligent Investor
paragraphs.cn: 聪明的投资者

paragraphs.en: Jason Zweig writes about investment strategy and how to think about money.
paragraphs.cn: 杰森·茨威格撰写投资策略和理财之道。

paragraphs.en: At least you can sell those. For most SPVs and similar pre-IPO vehicles, no secondary market exists. Some vehicles explicitly warn that you might never be able to sell. Other risks abound.
paragraphs.cn: 至少这些你可以出售。对于大多数SPV和类似的IPO前载体，不存在二级市场。有些载体明确警告你可能永远无法出售。其他风险也比比皆是。

paragraphs.en: DiCarlo says he would be happy to pay “a large one-time fee” to buy and hold such firms as Helion, Anthropic, Stripe, Cerebras or Groq—for as long as 10 to 25 years. But it’s virtually impossible to find an SPV or other vehicle that doesn’t assess many more costs than a one-time fee.
paragraphs.cn: 迪卡洛表示，他很乐意支付“一大笔一次性费用”来购买并持有Helion、Anthropic、Stripe、Cerebras或Groq等公司——持有时间长达10到25年。但实际上，几乎不可能找到一个除了收取一次性费用外，不评估更多成本的SPV或其他载体。

paragraphs.en: And while high costs are certain, future returns are a crapshoot.
paragraphs.cn: 尽管高成本是确定的，但未来的回报却是一场赌博。

paragraphs.en: Consider one offering, Series 62-1, from a firm called StartEngine. Series 62-1 was formed to invest in Grammarly, an online writing app. An unnamed affiliate of StartEngine bought Grammarly shares in September for the equivalent of $14 a share; Series 62-1 then agreed to buy them at $20.
paragraphs.cn: 考虑一下StartEngine公司的一项发行，名为Series 62-1。Series 62-1的成立是为了投资在线写作应用Grammarly。StartEngine的一家未具名关联公司在9月份以每股14美元的价格购买了Grammarly的股份；Series 62-1随后同意以20美元的价格购买这些股份。

paragraphs.en: So investors in Series 62-1 paid a 43% premium over the previous price. In addition, according to the offering document, the manager will take 20% of any future gains—and “may be entitled to receive” an annual management fee. The amount of that possible fee isn’t specified.
paragraphs.cn: 因此，Series 62-1的投资者支付了比前一价格高出43%的溢价。此外，根据发行文件，管理人将收取未来任何收益的20%——并且“可能有权收取”年度管理费。该可能费用的金额未具体说明。

paragraphs.en: Grammarly has since been renamed Superhuman. If it were to go public and hit $140 a share, or 10 times what the StartEngine affiliate paid, investors in Series 62-1 would make a sevenfold gain, and the manager would take a fifth of that along the way.
paragraphs.cn: Grammarly后来更名为Superhuman。如果它上市并达到每股140美元，即StartEngine关联公司支付价格的10倍，Series 62-1的投资者将获得七倍的收益，而管理人将从中抽取五分之一。

paragraphs.en: Unfortunately, industry executives say Superhuman is sparsely traded, with bids well below $15 a share.
paragraphs.cn: 不幸的是，行业高管表示Superhuman交易稀疏，出价远低于每股15美元。

paragraphs.en: StartEngine didn’t make anyone available to comment.
paragraphs.cn: StartEngine没有安排任何人对此发表评论。

paragraphs.en: “I’d be hard-pressed to think anybody would want an investment with so much upfront markup on it,” says Doug Kinsey, founding partner at Artifex Financial Group, an investment-advisory firm in Dayton, Ohio. “When your costs are this high, the odds are stacked against you.”
paragraphs.cn: 俄亥俄州代顿市投资咨询公司Artifex Financial Group的创始合伙人道格·金赛表示：“我很难想象有人会想要一项有如此高前期加价的投资。”“当你的成本如此之高时，成功的几率对你非常不利。”

paragraphs.en: Or consider a new offering from Republic, which enables you to “invest in the performance of SpaceX,” according to Republic’s app and website. The minimum investment is $100.
paragraphs.cn: 或者考虑Republic公司的一项新发行，根据其应用程序和网站，它使你能够“投资SpaceX的表现”。最低投资额为100美元。

paragraphs.en: The investment comes in the form of cryptocurrency tokens priced at $2 apiece. Their value, says Republic, should synthetically replicate, or “mirror,” the return on SpaceX shares. And how would that happen?
paragraphs.cn: 这项投资以加密货币代币的形式出现，每个代币定价2美元。Republic表示，它们的价值应该能够合成复制或“镜像”SpaceX股票的回报。那这会如何实现呢？

paragraphs.en: The proceeds of the offering, which seeks to raise up to $25 million, won’t go to purchase equity in SpaceX. Instead, you get an unsecured, 10-year debt obligation, with no regular interest payments, issued by a Republic affiliate.
paragraphs.cn: 这项旨在筹集高达2500万美元的发行所得，不会用于购买SpaceX的股权。相反，你将获得由Republic关联公司发行的一项无担保、10年期债务，没有定期利息支付。

paragraphs.en: The proceeds—after 23% is deducted for expenses—will fund “the further development of the tokenization business” of a Republic affiliate, says Hassan Asif, a Republic executive.
paragraphs.cn: Republic高管哈桑·阿西夫表示，扣除23%的费用后，所得款项将用于资助Republic关联公司“代币化业务的进一步发展”。

paragraphs.en: Then there’s a 3.5% one-time “administrative fee.”
paragraphs.cn: 此外，还有一笔3.5%的一次性“管理费”。

paragraphs.en: Each $2 token entitles you to a $1 position in the debt offering. In plain English, you’re paying a 100% entry charge.
paragraphs.cn: 每个2美元的代币使你在该债务发行中获得1美元的头寸。简单来说，你支付了100%的入场费。

paragraphs.en: Republic doesn’t explain exactly how it will invest so the tokens can mirror SpaceX’s shares, saying that its offering is “designed to reference the economic performance” of the stock.
paragraphs.cn: Republic没有确切解释它将如何投资以使代币能够镜像SpaceX的股票，只是说其发行“旨在参考该股票的经济表现”。

paragraphs.en: No wonder DiCarlo says, “I generally fear I’ll need a lawyer and an accountant if I sell a $50,000 investment 20 years from now, even if it ends up worth $5,000.”
paragraphs.cn: 难怪迪卡洛说：“我通常担心，如果我20年后出售一笔5万美元的投资，即使它最终只值5000美元，我也需要律师和会计师。”

paragraphs.en: For individual investors, the promise of this market is great. So far, the perils are even greater.
paragraphs.cn: 对于个人投资者来说，这个市场的潜力巨大。但到目前为止，风险甚至更大。

paragraphs.en: More from columnist Jason Zweig
paragraphs.cn: 专栏作家杰森·茨威格的更多文章

paragraphs.en: Appeared in the January 31, 2026, print edition as 'The High Price of These Hot Companies'.
paragraphs.cn: 刊登于2026年1月31日印刷版，标题为《这些热门公司的高昂代价》。

paragraphs.en: Jason Zweig writes The Intelligent Investor column every weekend for The Wall Street Journal. He also writes a weekly newsletter of the same name.
paragraphs.cn: 杰森·茨威格每周为《华尔街日报》撰写“聪明的投资者”专栏。他还撰写同名的每周通讯。

paragraphs.en: Jason is the author of “Your Money and Your Brain,” on the neuroscience of investing, and the editor of Benjamin Graham’s “The Intelligent Investor,” the classic text that Warren Buffett has described as “by far the best book about investing ever written.”
paragraphs.cn: 杰森是关于投资神经科学的《你的金钱与你的大脑》一书的作者，也是本杰明·格雷厄姆的经典著作《聪明的投资者》的编辑，沃伦·巴菲特曾将其描述为“迄今为止关于投资写得最好的一本书”。

paragraphs.en: Before joining the Journal, Jason helped the Nobel Prize-winning psychologist Daniel Kahneman write the book “Thinking, Fast and Slow.”
paragraphs.cn: 在加入《华尔街日报》之前，杰森曾协助诺贝尔奖得主心理学家丹尼尔·卡尼曼撰写《思考，快与慢》一书。

paragraphs.en: Earlier, Jason was a senior writer for Money magazine, a guest columnist for Time magazine and CNN.com, and a senior editor at Forbes magazine. He spent a year studying Middle Eastern history and culture at the Hebrew University in Jerusalem.
paragraphs.cn: 早些时候，杰森曾是《金钱》杂志的高级撰稿人、《时代》杂志和CNN.com的特约专栏作家，以及《福布斯》杂志的高级编辑。他曾在耶路撒冷的希伯来大学学习了一年的中东历史和文化。

vocabulary.meaning_en: Making something accessible to everyone; making something available to all people.
vocabulary.meaning_cn: 使普及；使民主化；使大众化
vocabulary.example: “I keep hearing about ‘democratizing’ access to private assets,” says John DiCarlo, a Wall Street Journal reader.
vocabulary.example_cn: 《华尔街日报》读者约翰·迪卡洛说：“我一直听说‘民主化’私人资产的获取途径。”

vocabulary.meaning_en: Investments that are not traded on public exchanges, such as private equity, venture capital, or real estate.
vocabulary.meaning_cn: 私人资产；非公开交易资产
vocabulary.example: “I keep hearing about ‘democratizing’ access to private assets,” says John DiCarlo, a Wall Street Journal reader.
vocabulary.example_cn: 《华尔街日报》读者约翰·迪卡洛说：“我一直听说‘民主化’私人资产的获取途径。”

vocabulary.meaning_en: The first sale of stock by a private company to the public, typically to raise capital.
vocabulary.example: DiCarlo, an engineer in the Washington, D.C., area, recently reached out to me because he has been trying—with no luck—for at least two years to buy a stake in private companies before they launch an initial public offering of their shares.
vocabulary.example_cn: 迪卡洛是华盛顿特区的一名工程师，他最近联系了我，因为他至少两年来一直试图在私营公司首次公开募股（IPO）前购买其股份，但都未能成功。

vocabulary.meaning_en: A situation in which a difficult choice has to be made between two or more alternatives, especially equally undesirable ones.
vocabulary.example: For me, his dilemma epitomizes both the potential and pitfalls of investing in private assets.
vocabulary.example_cn: 对我而言，他的困境集中体现了投资私人资产的潜力和陷阱。

vocabulary.meaning_en: Hidden or unsuspected dangers or difficulties.
vocabulary.example: For me, his dilemma epitomizes both the potential and pitfalls of investing in private assets.
vocabulary.example_cn: 对我而言，他的困境集中体现了投资私人资产的潜力和陷阱。

vocabulary.meaning_en: An investor who meets certain requirements regarding income, net worth, or professional experience, making them eligible to invest in certain securities that are not registered with regulatory bodies.
vocabulary.example: And you have to be a so-called accredited investor with at least $1 million in net assets, excluding your primary residence, or at least $200,000 in individual or $300,000 in joint income.
vocabulary.example_cn: 而且，你必须是一名所谓的“合格投资者”，拥有至少100万美元的净资产（不包括主要住所），或者个人年收入至少20万美元，或夫妻共同年收入至少30万美元。

vocabulary.meaning_en: A legal entity created to fulfill a specific, limited objective, often used in finance to isolate financial risk.
vocabulary.example: The most common form of access is often called a special-purpose vehicle.
vocabulary.example_cn: 最常见的获取方式通常被称为特殊目的载体（SPV）。

vocabulary.meaning_en: Charges or payments made at the beginning of a service, agreement, or transaction.
vocabulary.example: SPVs can bear upfront fees that could hit 5% to 12% or more, say industry executives.
vocabulary.example_cn: 行业高管表示，SPV可能收取5%到12%甚至更高的前期费用。

vocabulary.meaning_en: Gradually worn away, diminished, or destroyed. In a financial context, it means to be reduced or consumed by something, like fees.
vocabulary.example: “You better hope that company 10X’es or your return will be eroded away in fees,” he says.
vocabulary.example_cn: “你最好希望那家公司能增长10倍，否则你的回报就会被费用侵蚀殆尽，”他说。

vocabulary.meaning_en: A market where investors buy and sell securities they already own, rather than buying them directly from the issuing company.
vocabulary.example: For most SPVs and similar pre-IPO vehicles, no secondary market exists.
vocabulary.example_cn: 对于大多数SPV和类似的IPO前载体，不存在二级市场。
